content:
{
  "title": "Code-Switching Chain-of-Thought: Prompting LLMs with Mixed-Language Reasoning for Low-Resource NLP",
  "problem": "LLMs often underperform on multilingual tasks involving low-resource languages, especially when faced with code-switching—a common phenomenon in natural communication. Existing prompting strategies do not explicitly leverage code-switching as a cognitive bridge to improve LLM reasoning and generation for these languages.",
  "motivation": "Many speakers of low-resource languages naturally mix high-resource and low-resource languages in communication. Harnessing this phenomenon in LLM prompting could transfer knowledge from high-resource languages and facilitate better understanding and generation in low-resource contexts. As LLMs increasingly support global applications, new methods are needed to close the performance gap for underrepresented languages.",
  "method": "We propose a novel prompting method: Code-Switching Chain-of-Thought (CS-CoT), where few-shot exemplars are manually or automatically constructed to demonstrate stepwise reasoning that intentionally switches between a high-resource and a target low-resource language within each reasoning step. This code-switching not only provides linguistic scaffolding for the model but also encourages cross-lingual transfer at the reasoning level. CS-CoT prompts are compared against monolingual CoT and conventional code-mixed prompts without reasoning structure. An augmentation pipeline can auto-generate diverse code-switched reasoning exemplars by leveraging parallel corpora and translation models.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CS-CoT on multilingual QA and NLI datasets covering low-resource languages (e.g., MasakhaNER, XQuAD, FLORES-200). Baselines include zero-shot, monolingual CoT, and existing code-mixed prompts. Metrics include accuracy, F1, and qualitative analysis of reasoning coherence. Ablations will test the impact of code-switch directionality, frequency, and exemplar sourcing."
}
